commentBody:
Surprised to find no others here first. 

But regardless: It's a Monday. Fast and easy.
Please publish the column for browser access. Thank you!
Simple and fun Tuesday. Though I’ve only heard the term “chronically online” before. Could be used for a puzzle theme regarding physical ailments.
Not much to say about this one but here I am, saying it.

Suitable Tuesday I suppose.

Is it just me, or just an occupational hazard, or is there a surfeit of bog common fill lately?  It seems like every single day I'm entering Ogre, Saga, Iron, Pest, Host, Agrees, etc. etc.  This one seemed a bit more filly than usual.

The theme was okay.

Aah, a nice crunchy week, it seems like. To beginners, I say: Don't be discouraged. Being challenged is in keeping with the NYT gold standard. Expect it always, and you'll never get angry nor disappointed. Conversely, if it feels easy, revel in it – you're getting the hang of it, or you're getting better, or you're getting smarter, or you're on the constructor's wavelength, or Will Shortz loves you specifically, or any combination thereof. Thank you, Mr. Pandey, for the crunch and the many times I had to back up and retry and re-enter and skip and go back. Fun to see Shibuya Crossing which I happened to know because of this very recent (not so fun) article I read: https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/mar/12/japan-butsukari-otoko-bumping-man-trend-explained-tokyo-girl-shoved?CMP=share_btn_url And here is a live-cam of the crossing, for those not yet familiar with it: https://worldcams.tv/japan/tokyo/shibuya-crossing
Please please could these get a little harder … in this world of pain, the NYT crossword brings joy. And I liked this one but wasn’t ready for it to end so soon!
They should have switched yesterday’s puzzle with today’s… this is a Monday and that was a Tuesday!

The constructor's note was delicious!

Puzzle wasn't bad, either. Fun and enjoyable.

Thanks, Kiran!

Quick and easy, easier than Monday. Wish it'd had a little more meat on its bones, although I did enjoy the cookies. Ever notice how liverwurst and ONIONBAGEL have the same number of letters? (Thought not.) Gotta get back to a past Spelling Bee that has me deTERMInedlyONLINE. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DXjSVmz0IT0

Easy and breezy. Thankful the Midi quenched my thirst for a St. Patrick’s Day themed grid. I guess YODA and OGRE are both green? Thank you Kiran. Happy St. Patrick’s Day, Crosslandia. Enjoy this snippet of the Gardiner Brothers https://youtube.com/shorts/j-Wa-BzsQfE?si=zdI9Hb35l3iJQqt-
I found this one quit fun, thanks! Loved the TADA TYVM corner.

At about the 45 second mark you'll see what makes Fran Tarkenton such a memorable QB for the Super Bowl era Minnesota Vikings.

And here's some stats on his three Super Bowl appearances.

Super Bowl XIII: 18 for 28, 182 yds, 0 td, 1 int, rating 67.9
Super Bowl IX:  11 for 26, 102 yds, 0 td, 3 ints, rating 14.1
Super Bowl XI:  17 for 35, 205 yds, 1 td, 2 ints, rating 52.746

Sum:  46 of 89, 489 yds, 1 td, 6 ints in three games.

I wasn't a Minnesotan at the time, but I doubt the ratio of 6 to 1 in interceptions to touchdowns thrilled anybody.
Not really sure how they saw this week's Monday and Tuesday puzzles and landed on their respective days... Dead simple puzzle compared to yesterday's.

I don’t think STOIC means what people think it means. 

As I was going along I thought this is a nice Tuesday puzzle and then the star came and the app said I had been quicker than normal. Quite a bit quicker. I am becoming somewhat stoical about it.

In the Wordplay column, Kiran Pandey says, "One of the first things you learn as a crossword constructor is that it’s important for your puzzle to pass the [breakfast test.] That’s why I made sure to include lots of answers like SAUSAGE LINK, ONION BAGEL and SYRUP in this grid."

The constructor forgot to mention GIRLSCOUTCOOKIES. As far as I'm concerned, if they're in the house, they're breakfast, especially Thin Mints.

I'm a seven-month newbie and this was two days in a row without lookups! Yay!
TYVM for the achievable puzzle. TYSM for…nothing :)
My first 100 streak. Woohoo!

Tonight we watched the most recent Elspeth. Murder at the crossword convention!! 

What a freaking delight!!  I'm generally a fan of Elsbeth th and her Columbo like approach to things, but the whole crossword puzzle connection was delightful!! 

I think it was @Lynn that first drew my intention to it. How fun! Remind me so much of these crossword comments... It was hilarious!!
51D/54A had me turning to professor google but otherwise a fun Tuesday

SOFA as I'm concerned, an ONION_BAGEL hasn't ONION enough to give you pungent breath. SAUSAGELINKs, otoh....
In LIU of either

Oh the IRONY of thinking that little milk vetch plant was ASTRALagus! I understand it grows well in TOPS OIL...

Merlin's autobiography: I, MAGE. 
The sequel, while in higher ed: GRE, O, GRE!

Don't DUNST sound like it ought to be some kind of portmanteau verb? Like halfway between MUSTN'T and DAREN'T?

Is it true that a ROBIN sings at dawn? I thought that was a rooster or crow, though that might also be a COCKTAIL.

I'd love to TOKYO at face value but can't AGREE about Hospital concern being HEALTH, when focus was always Disease care more than HEALTH Care. Of late, they're all shifting toward being TERMINALLYbottomLINE
/sliding softly into endrant so y'all don't have to interCEDE

Loved the GLORIouS fill of ALLTHUMBS  and AVANTGARDE, for non-downer Downs to balance  the excellent Across themers. Appreciating the IRON-clad IRONY of BLOGPOST ending: HOST MEETS PEST

TY VEERy Much, @Kiran Pandey, I enjoyed it SHO 'nuff!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Apropos of nothing at all:  slow day at work and just happened upon a Weintraub Friday from 18 November 2022.

Sheer delight.

Her puzzles don't even need to be particularly difficult in order to elate, so delicious is her cluing.
I have finally figured it out: “Smoothie” must be the new nickname for “Crossword Puzzle”.
Surely 'ola' is a Spanish greeting? Portuguese say 'oi'. ??

So, as per usual, saved the revealer as the last string to fill in, paused the puzzle, and tried to figure out what the four theme answers had in common--
--things sold door-to-door, or at stands in public parks? Well, SAUSAGE LINKs not so much, although my lysergic acid diethylamide kiosk ("Lovin' by Lucy") does a brisk business.
--things consumed for breakfast? Once again, HIGH PROFILE doesn't fit the set.
--Oh, the *last* word in the entry!

Did anyone watch Jeopardy! last night? One answer/question directly related to Sunday and Monday's puzzles.

If you're looking for more of a challenge today, here's what's described as a "moderately challenging" puzzle by Erik Agard, from today's New Yorker: https://www.newyorker.com/puzzles-and-games-dept/crossword/2026/03/17

My new challenge every morning is finding the MIDI. I'm solving using Chrome on a MAC. Sometimes it's on the menu, sometimes it's not. Then, it's there, but alas, "Page no longer exists". I thought it was discontinued, but it's hiding somewhere.

Nice little brain pings as I uncovered the grid, such as when the clue for TOKYO shot me into imagining the world’s busiest traffic crossing, or when the gorgeous term for film and video – “moving image” – a term I haven’t thought of in a long time – appeared.

The beauty continued.  Not only in answer – STOIC, ODDITY, AVANTGARDE -- but also in the lush lineup of loveliness embedded in the clues: “squirt”, “unflinching”, “pungent”, “sprawling”, “relinquish”, “cosmic”, and “snippet”.  Wow!

Standouts such as themes, clever clues, and zingy answers are often the stars of a puzzle, but the less easily noticed supporting cast of images and memories that clues and answers ping, and splendid words, add richness.  I certainly felt that richness today.

So, much delight in the box for me, Kiran.  You elevated my day, and thank you!
It’s a shame that clue #10 across on the FIFA world cup quarter finals does not distinguish men vs women.
SOCKTAIL could maybe be something old fashioned, right? Nah, probly not ...

Took me just about as long as yesterday's puzzle, but enjoyed this one less. Didn't find the thematic entries or the revealer all that interesting,

Must have gotten ATSTAKE entirely from the crosses, since when I looked at my completed puzzle I couldn't parse it, and I wondered if it might be some variety of Japanese mushroom.

Not much green in today's St. Patrick's Day puzzle---most FIRS and many an OGRE, I suppose.

A fun one today! I do wish we had some kind of St. Patrick’s Day theme, but oh well. 🍀

In a wonderful bit of synchronicity, I just started reading Wordsworth’s “The Prelude” yesterday. How APT!

Happy Tuesday!

MIDI related comment: Seems a bit harsh to be catching strays over our world cup history on a paddy’s day theme.

Related PSA, not relevant to the puzzles: no one here would ever say Patty’s day, Patty is short for Patricia. Paddy is short for Patrick. (We’re mostly over worrying about being called Paddies as an insult. Mostly. Depends on who says it.)
Youth representative here, 32 across should be TYSM (thank you so much). 🫶🏻

This comment has nothing to do with today's puzzle, but rather because I saw this video about a word that appears commonly in the puzzle: DEKE. DEKE, which is short for "decoy," is almost always clued as a hockey term, and it seems that it got its origin in that sport. But in previous discussions, I've mentioned that the word is often used in other sports with the same notion: a player pretends to be doing one thing in order to fake out an opponent, who makes a mistake based on what he thinks he sees. Here's the video, from a few days ago at the World Baseball Classic: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L6rRJAqQQ7E

Breezy solve, with nothing that stood out one way or another…except that the revealer makes absolutely no sense to me.

How can “terminally online” possibly be a “description of the ends [of the theme answers]”?

I could see it if the revealer had dropped “the ends of”, and instead just said “a description of [the theme answers]”. In that case, “terminally” would be pointing you to the end/terminus of each theme answer.

However, as written, the revealer specifically instructs us to read “terminally online” as describing “the ends” of the theme answers…and while all of those things obviously relate to being “online”, where does “terminally” fit in?

No disrespect to Amy Virshup. I'm sure she's a fine travel writer and is probably helping out at the Games section with no pay bump for the extra work.

But did it seem to anyone else that she was just "phoning it in" today?

I suggested jokingly a few months ago that the Times could find willing and able columnists with ample knowledge and wit by scanning the Comments section here.
Now I think that it's time the puzzle editors looked at that suggestion a little more seriously.

Nominations are now open for visiting Wordplay columnist!
Thanks for tihis unusually fun and clever puzzle, for any day, especially So early in the week.

A bit on the slow side for a Tuesday but managed to work it all out. And... must confess that I remained a bit puzzled about the theme even after I was done. I guess I get it now, but just seems a bit odd to me. No big deal. And, of course, puzzle find today. A Monday from October 28, 2019 by the great Zhouquin Burnikel. Three straightforwardly clued theme answers: TAKEAGANDER ARIANAGRANDE COVENTGARDEN And then the 'reveal': "Safe.. or how the last words of 16-, 23- and 49-across are made?" OUTOFDANGER Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=10/28/2019&g=10&d=D ....
Came in 31 seconds shy of my Tuesday best on this one. Found it to be easier than Monday's puzzle this week.

ROBINs....the subject of many mistaken statements.  "Red, red ROBIN?"  Hardly.  They're brownish and orange-breasted; male and female are identical to the eye.  And they're not particularly known for dawn song.  A ROOSTER, now...  those are famous even for PRE-dawn crowing.  ROBIN's-egg blue...(not particularly unique in the world of birds'eggs.)  They don't go bob-bob-bobbin' along,  and their nests, constructed with considerable mud, have been known to slide or fall off an ill-chosen site, such as the angle of a drainpipe or a too-flimsy limb--(both witnessed by we ourselves.)
Okay, that about covers the GLORIES of robinhood.  

What a TEES, eh?
Every Spring, someone just has to remind me of my year as COOKIE Mom...
TYVM, Kiran.

[At Disneyland or the DMV, say]











TERMINALLY IN LINE

userLocation: another beautiful day in west marin!
commentBody:
fun!

loved “terminally online”

happy tuesday, everyone!

One more puzzle find - just kind of stumbled across this one. A Sunday from January 26, 1986 by Bert Rosenfield with the title: "Doubled headers" Some theme answers: MIRRORMIRRORONTHEWALL OVEROVERTHERE GOINGGOINGGONE REDREDROBIN NEVERNEVERLAND MANYMANYMOONS NONONANETTE TOOTOOSOLID And then a pair of linked answers: DODOTHATVOODOOTHATYOU DOSOWELL Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=1/26/1986&g=64&d=A ...

This required more little gray cells than usual for a Tuesday -- and I applaud the relative challenge.  This was because of such later-week cluing as the clues for ON IT (2D); AT STAKE (20A); IRONY (48D) and OIL (44A).  Now, as all of you know by now, I don't drive, so my knowledge of the care and feeding of cars is pretty much non-existent.  But I would have thought that the ol' gas-guzzler would require more than a "little squirt" of OIL.

My only writeover was Kirsten DUrST -- which kept me from seeing AVANT-GARDE for a while.  Re YODA: By the time I have fully completed my lifetime of doing the NYTXW, I will know everything there is to know about this franchise despite having walked out of the first film after about 50 minutes (not nearly soon enough!!!) and having never gone back for any other the films.  This YODA guy (a male, I assume) sure talks funny, doesn't he?

A Tuesday that I enjoyed quite a bit.

FWIW I didn't find today's puzzle easier than yesterday's. Bottom half of the puzzle took a bit of work on the crosses for me. XWSTATS agrees with me -- 6:33 average solve time yesterday versus 6:36 today (not that that's statistically signficant, mind you!). Just another reminder that one person's solving experience isn't necessarily the same as that of anyone else.
I found this a very fun puzzle! Thank you, Kiran Pandey!

Good puzzle today; thank you, Kiran Pandey! Bit of a relief after yesterday, when I discovered I do not know my Latin phrases very well. Ah well; I learned some for next time. :-)

I’m definitely not TERMINALLY ONLINE, I do not engage in any social media, unless the comments section counts as a platform. I prefer to keep a low profile. I feel I’m become one of the dinosaur generation; hear me roar.

I have joined the crowds over the Shibuya crossing. TOKYO has to be one of the most crowded cities I’ve ever been in, and I lived in London for a few years. Loved Japan and dream of returning one day.

A chewier than average Tuesday offering I felt, but nothing wrong with that.
It took me way too long to realize I had Sobin instead of Robin. I don't listen to Beach Boys, so for all I knew, they sang about Shonda lol.

One more late puzzle find. A Tuesday from June 28, 2005 by Jim Hyres. Four 15 letter grid-spanning across answers, evenly spaced from top to bottom. TWOEGGSOVEREASY FOURLEAFCLOVERS SIXFIGUREINCOME EIGHTTRACKTAPES Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/28/2005&g=31&d=A ....

As a very longtime solver, I haven’t noticed such words appearing more than usual. There will always be common fill, but the alternative, which was the rule when I started out, is worse:  uncommon fill that is so uncommon that people will not recognize it, even after it is revealed. 

Ports on remote Philippine islands, minor rivers in Asia Minor,supporting characters in Aeschylus plays, third-string outfielders from Babe Ruth’s era. These are the kinds of words you’ll see if the era of ERA comes to an end.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Andrzej

Jungian synchronicity strikes again.

So you're saying it's not an anarcho-syndicalist conspiracy with reptilian undertones?

You're part of it, aren't you...

That changes nothing. I still don't get it.

Trying to understand an American sport is like attempting to interpret alien mating rituals: what is *that*? Why would you do *this* with it? Why on Alpha Draconis VII would *beige* work best here? They said *what* now? Why are there *seven* of them here? Why do they need that guy outside the huddle? That's *not* what a leg is for!

Compare any American sport to football (as in, soccer): in the US you have several dozen guys taking positions, crouching, occasionally running, yards are being counted, the teams move around the field back and forth like on a chess board. And then in soccer some guys run around trying to kick the ball into the opponent's net. The only technicality that matters, really, is the off-side rule. That's it. Does it get any simpler than that?

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Andrzej

I'm not saying I am and I'm not saying I'm not...comrade.

@Andrzej
I must commend you on your fandom one of my favorite dark comedies of all time. I believe I've rewatched it 4 times since it first aired. Everything about it is just... wrong. Probably what makes it the guilty pleasure to beat all (pun on Nancy Botwin's proclivity to be roughly shagged)

@Jerry

That's the perfect way to describe it - everything *is* wrong about it, isn't it? You've put into words something I've been feeling myself but never knew how to express. Thanks!

Come to think of it... isn't that show like a twisted take on "The Simpsons"? Saturnalia at Springfield, eh?

Also, Andy Botwin has to be one of the most tragic characters in fiction - Jenji Kohan matched Aeschylus or Euripides there.

@Andrzej
Not only is it wrong, it's just rude, crude and socially deviant. I love it!

I got more of a twisted take on The Sopranos out of it.
Andy's character was captivating in several ways. He was naive, people pleasing, yet so positive and upbeat, he failed at almost everything. Tragic character described him the best.

It's funny that Breaking Bad aired 3 years (2008) after Weeds (2005), both with similar themes.

I've never seen "Breaking Bad". I never liked anything I read or heard about it, and I'm very picky when it comes to watching shows. For some reason, even though I have loads of spare time, I just can't stand wasting it on TV I don't like, of all things. I will never forgive myself for watching two seasons of Game of Thrones...

However, you might remember from one of our previous exchanges that I loved "Better Call Saul."

As for social deviancy, I feel a certain affinity to it. Don't get me wrong - I like social rules that enable people to live without harming one another too much. I can't stand people littering, cutting lines, failing to sort their trash, being nasty to retail workers, shouting at night, doing loud construction work on the weekend, etc. Yet at the same time I couldn't care less about most of the rites and rituals many people seem to value so highly - big weddings, anything religious, team-building exercises, secret santa: just, no. In that I am much closer to the Botwin gang than to members of polite society.

Also, I'm rude and crude most of the time.

@Steve L
Sooo many men named Francis!  Think St. Francis of Assisi, Francis Bacon, etc.  Men spell it Francis, women spell it Frances.
*No secret handshake to be seen here*

@dutchiris

I think Steve meant that he finds “Fran” to be an unusual nickname for a man, not that Francis is a weird name. Come to think of it, I’m not sure I’ve heard of a male Fran, aside from Tarkenton… although a female Frankie pops from time to time.

commentBody:
@Andrzej

Entirely.
@Veevee -- Interesting. My Brazilian friends say 'Oie', and they come variously from Bahia, Sao Paulo and Rio (pronounced 'Hee-yo'). Go figueroa.
@Curlew As a portuguese (born and living in Lisbon), "oi" is very informal and you wouldn't say it normally - besides, it's definitely more of a brazilian portuguese term.

@Bill

The judges did not expect the Latin.  That pause was priceless.

@Bill -- I, who like you, like to keep that revealer blank and try to guess what it is.  I got as far as realizing that it had to do with the last words of the theme answers, and was computer-related, but then, after much forehead wrinkling, I uncle-d. 

My brain loved the effort, though.

@SL
Took me half again as long as Patrick Berry's Monday puzzle, which was described as "challenging." (I usually find Monday NYers quite challenging.)

For those unfamiliar, the New Yorker runs puzzles Mon.-Wed., in *decreasing* levels of difficulty--the puzzles stay posted for a week--and limits itself to handful of seasoned constructors, whose likenesses caricature well; solving Robyn Weintraub's "beginner-friendly" Wednesdays can be a treat. Alas, no Happy Music upon completion, just silence.

(I see last Tuesday's puzzle was by Chandi Deitmer, one of my favorites. I must now go that.)

Bill,
Why should only The Times mix up difficulty days? Erik's took me a bit longer than Patrick's too. Both puzzles would be worthy Fridays of ELD here.

@Niall

Here, an old name for a police van is a paddy wagon, supposedly named because most of the cops were Irish, or because most of the criminals were Irish, depending on who you ask.

Patrick is usually just shortened to Pat here.  Patricia becomes Patty, Patti, or even Pattie.  Patty (or Patsy) might sometimes be short for Pasquale.

Happy St. Pat's*, as we sometimes say, to all who celebrate.

* Can also refer to the NYC cathedral.

@Lo Like others of my ancient vintage, I've noticed that "thank you very much," the natural standard in my native dialect, has almost totally capitulated to "thank you so much." The "so" version was always an option, but used only as a strong expression of appreciation for something extraordinary. Anyone have a sense of when this transition happened?

“the ends of the theme answers are the only part that relate to being online”

@D

Good enough for government work, eh?

Btw it’s not a “me” problem — unless someone can provide an explanation I’m missing, it’s a pretty straightforward gap in the revealer clueing.

And it seems very likely that the reason for the gap is the editors started out with “a description of [the theme answers]”, which would have made sense…but then decided that was a little too tough, and spliced in “the ends”, which doesn’t quite fit (as explained in my original comment).

But yeah, this is kind of a technicality that obviously won’t prevent anyone from solving the puzzle, or getting the *gist* of the theme revealer.

commentBody:
@Andrzej

@ad absurdum

Alternating paragraphs by @Lewis and @Andrzej.  I'd pay to read that!

@Andrzej
Thanks for recognizing my facetious comment. My admiration for your writings remain intact, as is my admiration for your dark humor.

@Rich in Atlanta And one more puzzle find - appropriate for today. A Sunday from March 16, 2003 by Elizabeth C. Gorski with the title: "Works like a charm!" A few answers straightforwardly clued: PEAGREEN GREENTEA GOLFGREEN GREENSPAN And then... hard to describe the center but it was a 3 x 3 block with some green squares. And one other pair of clues and answers was: "With 114-across, tune heard on the 37-down" IMLOOKINGOVER And... 114-across: "Centerpiece of this puzzle" FOURLEAFCLOVER And... two other long down answers: 37-down: FEASTOFSTPATRICK 16-down: THELUCKOFTHEIRISH Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=3/16/2003&g=37&d=D ....

@MOL --- Glad to see I'm not alone in suspecting that clue of ROBIN hoodwinking. 
Given those trees near my driveway, they seem to use my car as their Littlejohn.

@MOL
So your post got me wondering whether the European robins--*Erithacus rubecula*, a flycatcher--are redder than the American robins--*Turdus migratorius*, a thrush. They aren't. Do they bob-bob-bob more? 

But in looking that up, TIL that the original English name was simply "redbreast"; later, they started calling them "Robin Redbreast,"  with the (human, male) name added for cutesiness; then finally, just "robin." This the same way the original French word for "fox," *goupil*, became "Reynard the Fox," then finally Modern French "renard." (My Partner's surname is a variant on "renard," and I can assure you, he is quite foxy, in both senses of the word.)

*The Linnean name *Erithacus rubecula* comes from the words for "red," in Greek and Latin, respectively, so no confusion there!

@Andrzej
Well, as @MOL said, they're brownish.

@Mean Old Lady - I agree, mostly -- most robins are decidedly orange, no red, but there are regional variation, and some are quite red. There's a subspecies that breeds in eastern Canada that is more red -- it is sometimes seen in the winter where in New England. (Fun factoid*: robins are NOT a sign of spring on the East coast -- they're here year-round.) Robins in the PNW are often more red, too. 

I also agree about their song -- they sing all day, maybe more later in the day than the morning. But they do sing very early, usually before sunrise. But I associate larks and wrens more with the dawn -- and, as already mentioned -- roosters. 

*Sadly, I must now include a disclaimer for the pedants who feel the need to 'splain what I suspect most of us already know: a factoid is not really a fact. At least according to its etymology. 

I suspect such people also think the word "decimate" can refer ONLY to the specific situation where you are killing 10% of the soldiers in an army.

@Captain Quahogb

Robins, in folklore, literature, culture, or something are referred to as a harbinger of spring. And I, for one, am always glad to see the first robin and then I know that, whew, that terrible winter is over. 

I think I will go now and check outside for the "first" robin.  I've already seen my pretty purple crocuses showing their lovely flowers and my gazillions of daffodils are not far behind!

I have to admit that the worst thing about winter is paying for the heating!! But I certainly do drool for the start of gardening season and the sun on my back.

@lucky13 - Yes, I am aware that they are mythologized as a "harbinger of spring."

Nevertheless, the reality is that they are present all winter long on the entire East Coast -- even here in Maine, and certainly in New York. I have been hearing them sing for at least two weeks now -- here in the Great North Woods of Maine. But they were here before they started singing -- they're here all winter long.

@Mean Old Lady
I always assume red/orange confusion in naming things is because of the thing where there was no english word for orange until the fruit showed up in Europe. Absolutely no idea of that is the case here (or any other thing). Someone could well be about to tell that red/orange naming thing is just made up.

@Captain Quahog To really know which birds stick around in the winter, you can check out my MAGNUMOPUS--a paint by number painting I completed recently. @Matt Lee I have not had one single bit of trouble with the Midi since it debuted a few weeks ago, so I find it surprising that so many people are having these disappearances. Assuming you want suggestions, and not just are here to gripe, try the following: 1. Make sure your operating system and browser are up to date. 2. Check the Archive to see if the Midi puzzles are there. If they are, even today's puzzle should be there. 3. Restart your device. 4. Clear your cache. 5. Email the tech desk at NYTGames@nytimes.com. This address is at the top of the comments every day. Hope this helps!
